title: Chapter 7: The City Beneath the Dead
content: Greyhaven didn't announce itself. It *seeped*.

The entrance was a collapsed subway tunnel three miles east of the Ruins, hidden beneath a landslide of concrete and rusted rebar. No signs. No guards — at least, none awake during the day. Just a darkness that grew deeper and colder as Kael descended, until the tunnel walls changed from brick to carved stone to something older. Walls of compacted bone and fossil. The foundations of a city built before humans had learned to write.
And then the darkness broke, and Greyhaven opened around him.

It was massive. A cavern so vast the far walls were lost in shadow, the ceiling so high Kael couldn't see it through the haze of torch-smoke and blood-incense. Buildings rose from the stone floor in concentric rings — black iron spires, obsidian cathedrals, markets that sprawled across a dozen interconnected chambers. The whole city descended toward a central point where a single structure loomed above everything: the Keep, a fortress of fused bone and dark glass that looked like a ribcage turned inside out.

"That's Dredd's seat," Sera murmured. She'd insisted on coming despite her condition, despite the fact that every vampire in Greyhaven would smell the humanity on her. "Viscount Dredd. The Rot King. Two hundred years on that throne, and nothing to show for it but a city that's slowly dying."

Kael studied the Keep from the tunnel entrance. At this hour — three hours past sunset — the city was alive. Vampires moved through the streets in numbers he hadn't thought possible. Barons in long coats, fledglings in ragged clothes, thralls carrying trays of congealed blood. And humans. He saw humans — some walking with the hollow-eyed shuffle of the blood-bound, others moving freely, merchants and blacksmiths and whores who'd learned to survive in the belly of the beast.
"Where do we start?" he asked.

"The blood market. Level Three. If there's information to be had, that's where you'll find it. And if there's someone who can help us..." She paused. "There's a blacksmith. Marcus Thorne. Human. He's been down here twelve years, arming both sides of the war. If anyone knows the Keep's defenses, it's him."
Level Three was chaos.

The blood market sprawled across half a dozen caverns — stalls and tents and permanent stone shops selling everything from fresh blood vials to human servants to information about rival clans. The air was thick with copper and fear and the low thrum of a hundred simultaneous negotiations. Vampires haggled with vampires. Humans haggled with humans. The few who crossed the species line did so with the careful wariness of predators who weren't sure who was prey.

Kael moved through the crowd and the crowd moved around him. Baron-rank emanated from him like heat — the other vampires could feel it, could sense the power radiating from his newly-consolidated form. They gave him space without knowing why. Fledglings flinched. Thralls bowed. Even other Barons nodded with the grudging respect of peers who recognized a potential threat.
He found the blacksmith at the edge of the market.

Marcus Thorne was massive. Shoulders like a bull, arms thick with muscle and burn scars, hands that could crush stone but moved with surprising delicacy as he hammered a blade at his forge. The forge itself was a masterpiece of salvaged engineering — a furnace built from scrap iron, bellows made from old leather, tools that had clearly been maintained with obsessive care.
"You're a long way from the surface," Marcus said without looking up.
"I'm looking for information."

"Everyone's looking for information. Most of them are vampires. Most of them don't pay." He set down his hammer and turned. His eyes were grey, steady, unsurprised to see a Baron in his shop. "But you're different, aren't you?"
"How can you tell?"

"Your eyes. They're not hungry." Marcus crossed his arms. "Twelve years I've been down here. Twelve years of vampires treating me like furniture, like livestock, like a convenient source of custom weapons and emergency blood. Every single one of them has the same look. Hungry. Even the ones who've just fed. Even the ones who pretend to be civilized." He studied Kael's face. "You don't have that look. You have a soldier's look. A hunter's look. What were you before?"
"Silver Dawn. Third Platoon. Scout."
Marcus's expression shifted — surprise, then interest, then something that might have been respect. "A hunter turned hunter. That's rare. Most turned hunters kill themselves within the first week."
"I found a reason not to."

"The paladin." Marcus glanced at Sera, who was examining the weapons on his wall with professional interest. "Silver Dawn armor. Knight-Sergeant insignia. And..." He squinted at her eyes, at the faint golden glow. "You're not human anymore. Not entirely."
"Not entirely," Sera agreed. "But I'm not a vampire either. Consider me a work in progress."

"So you're the anchor. And you're the daylight Baron everyone's been whispering about." Marcus picked up his hammer. "The blood market's been buzzing for two days. A fledgling who killed a Baron at dawn. Who stood in sunlight and didn't burn. Half the city thinks it's a myth. The other half is terrified it's not."
"Good," Kael said. "Fear is useful."
"Fear's a double-edged sword. It keeps the small ones away. It draws the big ones closer." Marcus set the hammer down again. "What do you want from me?"
"Intel on the Keep. Guard rotations. Defensive measures. Dredd's personal habits. Every weakness you've noticed in twelve years of watching."
Marcus was quiet for a long moment. The forge fire crackled. Outside, the blood market continued its endless churn of commerce and predation.

"Why should I trust you?" he asked finally. "You're a vampire. You killed a Baron, sure, but that just makes you a stronger vampire. How do I know you're not going to become exactly like the rest of them the moment you have real power?"

Kael met his eyes. "Because I'm not asking you to trust me. I'm asking you to use me. You've been waiting twelve years for someone to come and tear this city down. Someone who could do what you couldn't. Someone who could kill Dredd and burn his rotten kingdom to ash."
"And you're that someone?"

"I'm the only vampire in the world who can walk into Dredd's throne room at noon. I'm the only one who can fight him while every guard in the Keep is unconscious. I'm the only one who can bring the sun underground." Kael stepped closer. "You don't have to like me. You don't have to trust me. You just have to tell me where to hit."
The silence stretched. Then Marcus laughed — a rough, surprised sound that seemed to shake the soot from the rafters.

"Twelve years," he said. "Twelve years of vampires treating me like furniture, and a former Silver Dawn scout walks into my shop and asks me to help him assassinate a Viscount." He picked up his hammer. "I'm in. The Keep has three rings of defense. Outer wall, inner sanctum, throne room. The guards change at dawn, but the day shift is light because Dredd thinks no one's stupid enough to attack during the day. And there's a structural weakness in the throne room ceiling — old damage from a cave-in fifty years ago that was never properly repaired."
Kael felt something click into place. A plan. A trajectory. A target.
"Show me," he said.
